articles.title: Confirm packet loss between SIP hops before changing application behavior
articles.summary: Packet loss on the signaling path is a common root cause for retransmission spikes and degraded service.
content: When to use: Retransmission count rises with latency and the same transactions succeed only after multiple attempts.
Action: Compare packet loss and error counters across the ingress, proxy, and downstream service hops involved in the incident.
Why it works: Loss between hops creates retry patterns that can be misread as application overload.
Verify: Retransmissions fall once the lossy segment is repaired or bypassed.
Evidence: Keep the hop-level loss comparison with the incident for future similarity search.

articles.title: Validate MTU and fragmentation after sudden transport instability
articles.summary: MTU mismatches can trigger intermittent signaling loss and odd parser behavior.
content: When to use: Larger SIP messages or media-related signaling paths fail more often than small control messages.
Action: Compare MTU settings across the path, inspect fragmentation counters, and test whether oversized packets are being dropped.
Why it works: Fragmentation defects create unpredictable delivery and can amplify retransmission storms.
Verify: Large message success improves and retransmissions no longer cluster around the same payload sizes.
Guardrail: Apply MTU changes consistently across the affected path instead of only one endpoint.

articles.title: Inspect DNS latency and cache health on degraded network paths
articles.summary: Slow DNS can make signaling appear degraded even when the transport itself is fine.
content: When to use: Transaction latency rises during name lookup or peer resolution stages without clear packet loss.
Action: Measure DNS lookup time, cache hit ratio, and resolver availability for the services on the incident path.
Why it works: Name-resolution delays often look like generic network slowness until the lookup step is isolated.
Verify: End-to-end latency improves when resolver health or caching is restored.
Aftercare: Add DNS latency as a first-class incident signal for similar network degradations.

articles.title: Verify QoS marking and priority on the signaling path
articles.summary: Misclassified traffic can suffer avoidable jitter or drop during congestion.
content: When to use: Network degradation appears during congestion windows and signaling competes poorly with less critical traffic.
Action: Check DSCP or equivalent QoS markings and confirm the network is honoring the expected priority for SIP signaling.
Why it works: Correct prioritization reduces delay and loss without changing application behavior.
Verify: Signaling latency and retransmission behavior improve during the same load profile.
Risk: Do not raise priority blindly if it would starve other critical control traffic.

articles.title: Move traffic off a saturated node or interface
articles.summary: Localized node or interface saturation can create network incidents with a narrow but intense blast radius.
content: When to use: One node, NIC, or host path shows much worse throughput, drops, or queueing than the rest of the fleet.
Action: Drain the saturated path, rebalance traffic to healthy nodes, and inspect the constrained interface separately.
Why it works: Removing the hot spot restores service faster than broad application changes.
Verify: Retransmission spikes follow the bad path out of service and healthy paths absorb traffic cleanly.
Evidence: Preserve node and interface counters so the issue is not later attributed to the wrong layer.

articles.title: Compare east-west network policy after a connectivity regression
articles.summary: A network-policy change can block or delay service-to-service signaling within the cluster.
content: When to use: Degradation started after a platform security change and only certain service-to-service paths are affected.
Action: Review recent network-policy updates, confirm the allowed ports and namespaces, and compare failing and healthy paths.
Why it works: Cluster policy mistakes can look like random latency or timeout issues until the blocked flow is identified.
Verify: Connectivity returns on the affected path and transaction timing normalizes without another service rollout.
Rollback trigger: A specific policy revision that aligns with the incident start.

articles.title: Tune retransmission thresholds only after confirming the path can sustain them
articles.summary: Overly aggressive retransmission behavior can magnify moderate network degradation into a larger incident.
content: When to use: Modest latency changes trigger disproportionately large retransmission waves from clients or peers.
Action: Review retransmission timers and thresholds together with the real latency envelope seen on the path.
Why it works: Better timer alignment reduces amplification while the underlying network issue is being stabilized.
Verify: Duplicate transaction volume drops and the path stops oscillating under moderate latency.
Guardrail: Do not mask a severe transport defect by stretching timers beyond safe user experience bounds.

articles.title: Confirm load-balancer health checks are ejecting unhealthy endpoints
articles.summary: If health checks lag reality, traffic can keep flowing to degraded endpoints and create network symptoms.
content: When to use: Network symptoms concentrate on one backend path even though the load balancer still reports it as healthy.
Action: Review health-check sensitivity, timeout values, and endpoint removal timing for the affected service.
Why it works: Fast and accurate health checks stop new traffic from hitting paths already known to be failing.
Verify: Traffic shifts away from the bad path and retransmission counts decrease on new transactions.
Risk: Overly aggressive health checks can flap, so tune them with observed path stability in mind.

articles.title: Inspect jitter and transport variance on media-gateway adjacent paths
articles.summary: High jitter can degrade signaling and session maintenance even without outright packet loss.
content: When to use: Latency variance is high, sessions feel unstable, and packet loss is not the dominant signal.
Action: Measure jitter and queueing behavior on the media-gateway and adjacent transport segments involved in the incident.
Why it works: Variance-driven degradation can trigger timeouts and poor session behavior before average latency looks critical.
Verify: Reduced jitter correlates with improved call stability and lower timeout volume.
Documentation: Include jitter evidence in the incident payload because average latency alone may hide the problem.

articles.title: Fail over to a healthy rack or zone when network degradation is localized
articles.summary: If the fault is isolated to one zone or rack, controlled failover can restore service quickly.
content: When to use: Most errors originate from one rack, availability zone, or network segment while the rest of the platform remains stable.
Action: Shift traffic to a healthy segment and reduce dependency on the degraded zone until network repair is complete.
Why it works: Localized failover limits user impact without requiring immediate application-level changes.
Verify: Traffic succeeds through the healthy segment and incident metrics stop following the degraded zone.
Risk: Confirm destination capacity and cross-zone policy allowances before initiating failover.
